sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, Larry, presents with chronic lumbar back pain that started 2 years ago after moving furniture, describing it as a sharp stabbing pain that radiates down to his right leg and even to his big toe.
He also reports numbness and tingling in his right leg, particularly in the lateral thigh at the L4 dermatome.
He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area and weakness.
He has tried physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past, but they were only temporary.
He also mentions that he has gained weight due to his back pain.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of trying various treatments for his back pain, including physical therapy and pain medications.
He also mentions that his primary care doctor referred him to see me for this issue.
He has a history of smoking, but quit 20 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient reports numbness and tingling in his right leg, but denies any loss of sensation in other areas or other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has no overlying erythema or ecchymosis on his back.
There is midline tenderness at L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
The patient experiences pain with lumbar flexion and a positive straight leg test.
The neurological examination shows decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
The patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results: The MRI shows disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy, as evidenced by his chronic lumbar back pain, radiating pain to his right leg, numbness, and tingling.
Problem list: L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy; chronic lumbar back pain; radiculopathy.
Plan:
I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate the patient's pain.
The corticosteroid will be injected into the lumbar spine under fluoroscopy.
I will monitor the patient's response to the injection and schedule follow-up appointments as needed.
Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up appointment in 1-2 weeks to assess his response to the injection and schedule additional injections as needed, up to a maximum of 4 times a year.
